albums.title: Pocket Symphony
albums.reason: With 1011 all-time plays, Air are deep in your DNA — Pocket Symphony is their most overlooked gem, a hushed, orchestral drift you probably haven't revisited in years.

albums.title: Senior
albums.reason: You've given Röyksopp over 1000 plays across your lifetime, and Senior — the darker, ambient companion to Junior — is exactly the kind of deep cut that rewards a long-overdue return.

albums.title: 100th Window
albums.reason: Massive Attack sit in your all-time top 50, and 100th Window is their most underrated and isolated record — cold, slow, and hypnotic in a way that feels built for late nights.

albums.title: Surrender
albums.reason: The Chemical Brothers have 556 all-time plays for you, and Surrender — with its wall-to-wall peak-era energy — is a record that hits differently when you come back to it after years away.
You've logged 551 plays of Moby, and 18 — the quieter, more reflective follow-up to Play — is the one most likely to have slipped from your regular rotation but still holds up beautifully.

albums.title: Demon Days
albums.reason: Gorillaz appear in your all-time top 50, and Demon Days is one of the great albums of that era — dense, cinematic, and overdue a proper revisit.

albums.title: Moderat
albums.reason: You've played Moderat tracks like Rusty Nails heavily, and their self-titled debut — gritty, tense, and more raw than the later albums — is the one that often gets skipped in favour of II and III.

albums.title: Animal Magic
albums.reason: Bonobo sit at 440 all-time plays and are still in your recent listening — Animal Magic, his earliest full statement, has a rawer, more organic warmth that's easy to forget about when the later records dominate.

albums.title: Singularity
albums.reason: You've engaged with Jon Hopkins via previous picks, and Singularity — his 2018 masterpiece that moves from crushing techno to beatless ambience — fits your taste for music that builds and breathes.

albums.title: Friends That Break Your Heart
albums.reason: James Blake is your 17th most-played artist of all time, and Friends That Break Your Heart is his most recent full statement — polished, emotional, and slightly underplayed compared to his earlier records.

albums.title: Silk Road
albums.reason: Maribou State have 434 all-time plays and appear in your recent tracks too — Silk Road sits right in the late-2010s vault zone and is fuller and more layered than Kingdoms in Colour.

albums.title: Obverse
albums.reason: With 560 all-time plays, Trentemøller is a long-term obsession, and Obverse — his most recent album — is a return to the moody, cinematic darkness of The Last Resort that feels right for a revisit.

albums.title: Autumn Bells
albums.reason: If Röyksopp's glacial Nordic atmosphere and Air's quiet orchestral restraint are in your all-time DNA, Swedish duo Gidge deliver exactly that — hushed, yearning, and criminally overlooked.

albums.title: Flying Away
albums.reason: Your deep love of Massive Attack and Portishead's era points straight here — Smoke City's 1997 debut fuses bossa nova and trip-hop in a way that felt ahead of its time and still sounds stunning.

albums.title: Sort of Revolution
albums.reason: Your affinity for artists like James Blake and The Shins who blend emotional directness with sonic texture makes Fink's Sort of Revolution a natural fit — deeply felt, minimal, and quietly powerful.

albums.title: Silver Wilkinson
albums.reason: Your all-time love of Boards of Canada's textured nostalgia and Aphex Twin's warmer moments finds a kindred spirit in Bibio's Silver Wilkinson — sun-dappled, gentle, and surprisingly deep.

albums.title: Krieg und Frieden (Music for Theatre)
albums.reason: Your all-time devotion to artists who merge electronics with something grand — Daft Punk, Radiohead, Underworld — makes this Apparat orchestral album an ideal discovery: harrowing, huge, and beautiful.

albums.title: The Late Sleepers
albums.reason: Fans of Plaid, Aphex Twin, and Orbital's more introspective side often find Mr. Projectile's 2003 record to be a perfect lost companion — intricate, warm, and just off the beaten path.

albums.title: Never Fade Away
albums.reason: Your recent arc through Sault, Charles Bradley, and Curtis Mayfield shows you're drawn to music with deep soul and social weight — Baloji's 2023 album is visionary, textured, and utterly alive.

albums.title: Perspective
albums.reason: Your recent listening to Dominik Eulberg and Four Tet's rhythmically adventurous electronic work opens a door to Jlin — whose 2024 album is percussive, thrilling, and like nothing else around.

albums.title: Phases
albums.reason: Your recent deep dive into Sault and Erykah Badu suggests a taste for Black music with spiritual confidence and sonic boldness — Moonchild Sanelly's Phases hits that same nerve, hard.

albums.title: Cirklar
albums.reason: Your recent heavy listening to Oscar Peterson Trio, Bill Evans, and Portico Quartet points toward this Swedish piano trio — melodically rich, emotionally direct, and deeply considered.

albums.title: Becoming Air
albums.reason: Portico Quartet sit near the top of your recent listening, and Norwich trio Mammal Hands occupy a very similar space — saxophone-led, rhythmically alive, and suffused with the same patient beauty.

albums.title: Uomo Donna
albums.reason: Your recent love of Khruangbin's hazy global warmth and Bill Withers' soulful directness finds an unexpected Italian cousin here — orchestral, heartfelt, and rich with the same human warmth.

albums.title: Mordechai Remixes
albums.reason: Khruangbin are dominating your last week and month, and this remix collection takes the warm, slow-motion pocket you've been living in and stretches it into even more hypnotic, dub-influenced territory.

albums.title: Mirage
albums.reason: Golden Times 1 has been on repeat for you this week, and Mirage — a companion record of quiet, shimmering pieces — sits in the same luminous, unhurried space and rewards the same deep listening.

albums.title: Art in the Age of Automation
albums.reason: Ruins has been a constant for you lately, and Art in the Age of Automation — the album it comes from — is a perfectly sequenced journey that deserves to be heard as a whole right now.

albums.title: Veil
albums.reason: You played a Parra for Cuva track just this week, and Veil — his full-length debut — has the same warm, slow-dissolving electronic texture that fits perfectly where your head is right now.

albums.title: Our Love
albums.reason: You've just been listening to Caribou's Swim, and Our Love is the warmer, more intimate companion to it — still hazy and layered, but with a softness that matches your current mood perfectly.

albums.title: Phantom Brickworks
albums.reason: Bibio appeared in your recent tracks with Lovers' Carvings, and Phantom Brickworks is his most stripped-back and meditative record — long, slow, and ideal for the patient listening space you're in.

songs.title: The Saint
songs.reason: Orbital have 777 all-time plays in your history, and The Saint — from In Sides — is one of their most emotionally soaring and underplayed moments, the kind of track that earns a full-volume revisit after years away.

songs.title: Caring Is Creepy
songs.reason: The Shins are in your all-time top 50 and this track — the haunting opener of Oh, Inverted World — is a pure 2000s time capsule that probably hasn't crossed your ears in a long time.

songs.title: Where's Your Head At
songs.reason: Basement Jaxx have 496 all-time plays for you, and this Rooty cut is a stone-cold early 2000s anthem that belongs back in your ears — wild, manic, and completely joyful.

songs.title: Just a Day
songs.reason: Feeder sit at 768 plays in your all-time list, and Just a Day is one of their most emotionally direct moments — short, urgent, and a perfect snapshot of early 2000s British indie.

songs.title: Original
songs.reason: Leftfield are in your all-time top 10 with over 1000 plays, and Original — from Leftism — is one of their most hypnotic and subterranean tracks, perfect for rediscovering in full.

songs.title: Jumbo
songs.reason: With 633 all-time plays, Underworld are a cornerstone of your taste, and Jumbo from Beaucoup Fish is one of their most emotionally soaring moments — it deserves to come back around.

songs.title: Dreamer
songs.reason: Four Tet are in your recent 12-month listening and New Energy sits in your recent albums — Dreamer is one of its most purely beautiful moments, a mid-tempo glowing track worth singling out.

songs.title: Recovery
songs.reason: Rival Consoles appear across your data, and Recovery from Persona is one of their most focused and emotionally charged tracks — driven, insistent, and landing right in the late-2010s sweet spot.

songs.title: Therapy
songs.reason: You've played Rusty Nails heavily from Moderat, and Therapy — from III — is one of their most vulnerable and quietly devastating songs, sitting right in that medium-term past window.

songs.title: Familiar
songs.reason: Nils Frahm sits at 64 plays in your last 12 months, and Familiar — from All Melody — is one of his most orchestrally grand and emotionally open tracks, quite different from the solo piano pieces you know best.

songs.title: No Reason
songs.reason: Bonobo are a consistent presence in your data, and No Reason from Migration is one of their most purely uplifting tracks — a reminder of how high that 2017 album could soar.

songs.title: Midas
songs.reason: Maribou State have strong all-time play counts for you, and Midas — from Silk Road — is one of their most emotionally resonant and underheard tracks, a perfect fit for the recent vault.

songs.title: Time Is the Enemy
songs.reason: Your all-time love of Massive Attack's orchestral trip-hop weight and Stevie Wonder's soul warmth makes Quantic's string-drenched soul a natural discovery — lush, melancholic, and deeply felt.

songs.title: Superstar
songs.reason: If Massive Attack and Portishead are cornerstones of your all-time taste, Dobie's Superstar is exactly the kind of overlooked late-90s trip-hop gem that should have been as big — haunting and perfectly weighted.

songs.title: If I Survive
songs.reason: Your all-time devotion to The Chemical Brothers and Underworld's euphoric electronic scale makes Hybrid an obvious discovery — If I Survive is a cinematic, string-swept peak that stands with the best of that era.

songs.title: Arterial
songs.reason: Your all-time taste in precise, emotional electronic music — Plaid, Aphex Twin, Orbital — points toward Lusine's Arterial: intricate, cool-toned, and satisfying in exactly the same structural way.

songs.title: Ljoss
songs.reason: Your all-time taste bridges shoegaze, electronic, and something atmospheric and heavy — Forest Swords' Ljoss is exactly that: ghostly, densely textured, and unlike almost anything else.

songs.title: Linden
songs.reason: Fans of Plaid, Aphex Twin, and Orbital's more introspective side often find Mr. Projectile to be a perfect lost companion — Linden is intricate, warm, and sits in exactly the same headspace as your all-time favourites.

songs.title: Dear Sikhemba
songs.reason: Your recent arc through Oscar Peterson, Bill Evans, and Portico Quartet's jazz-adjacent sounds opens a door to Nduduzo Makhathini — a South African pianist whose music is rooted in something ancient and deeply felt.

songs.title: Last Night
songs.reason: Your recent months of Hania Rani, Ólafur Arnalds, and A Winged Victory for the Sullen show a taste for music that is hushed and emotionally enormous — Arooj Aftab's Last Night is exactly that, with a vocal performance that stops time.

songs.title: The Sound of Being Alone
songs.reason: Your recent heavy listening to Nils Frahm, Joep Beving, and Hania Rani traces a clear line to Sven Helbig's piano and string minimalism — The Sound of Being Alone is one of his most quietly devastating tracks.

songs.title: Tótem
songs.reason: Your recent love of Khruangbin's global, unhurried groove and Dominik Eulberg's richly textured electronic work makes Nicola Cruz a natural discovery — Tótem is earthy, hypnotic, and deeply transportive.

songs.title: Rites
songs.reason: Your recent listening to Portico Quartet and Yussef Dayes signals a hunger for jazz that doesn't sit still — Ill Considered's Rites is exactly that: recorded live in one take, urgent, and utterly free.

songs.title: Winter Flowers
songs.reason: Your recent deep listening to Bill Withers, Erykah Badu, and Charles Bradley shows a strong pull toward soul music with emotional honesty at its centre — Yazmin Lacey's Winter Flowers hits that same nerve, beautifully.

songs.title: If There Is No Question
songs.reason: You've been circling Khruangbin's catalogue all week, and If There Is No Question — from A La Sala — captures exactly the slow, floating groove that's clearly where you are right now, with even more space and stillness than usual.

songs.title: Memory Streams
songs.reason: Ruins has been on repeat for you, and Memory Streams — the title track from their most recent album — shares the same slow emotional build and luminous suspended quality that's been defining your listening this week.

songs.title: Strands
songs.reason: Golden Times 1 keeps appearing in your most recent plays, and Strands — from the Spells album — has the same patient, glowing texture and quiet emotional weight that makes it a perfect companion this week.

songs.title: Anything New
songs.reason: You played Bibio's Lovers' Carvings this week, and Anything New — another Ambivalence Avenue highlight — has the same woozy, warm glow that fits the gentle, inward mood you're clearly in.

songs.title: Incapable
songs.reason: Murphy's Law just appeared at the top of your recent tracks, and Incapable — from Róisín Machine — is one of her most joyful and sonically rich moments, a worthy next step in that same session.

songs.title: You and I
songs.reason: You played Caribou's Sun this week from Swim, and You and I — the opening track — is one of the most disarmingly beautiful things on that record, worth pulling out on its own right now.

djSets.setName: Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
Rodriguez Jr. b2b Arodes - DISTRIKT - Burning Man 2025
Feathers & Bones - Mixtape #04 [February 2026] - Hybrid Live @ Balance Festival Croatia 2025
